text: Status epilepticus refers to:

text: To prepare a completely amputated part of the body for transportation to hospital with the casualty, you should:
optionA: Place it in a clean plastic bag filled with cold water.
optionB: Wrap it in clean, moist dressings and keep it cool.

optionD: Wrap it in clean, moist dressings and keep it at body temperature.

text: Hypovolemic shock results from:
optionA: The body's reaction to a foreign protein.
optionC: Toxins produced by a severe infection.

text: A conscious casualty who has suffered a stroke becomes unconscious. To keep his airway open and help him breathe, you should:
optionA: Turn him into the recovery position with his unaffected side down.
optionB: Place him in a semisitting position.
optionC: Turn him into the recovery position with his unaffected side up.

text: A worker has fallen from a six-foot ladder onto a concrete floor. You first action should be.
optionC: Take charge and perform a scene survey.

text: To determine whether a c-spine injury has occurred, you should think about the Mechanism of Injury. This can best be described as:

optionD: The area of the body that is injured

text: The most critical factor in defibrillation is:

text: When using an AED on a patient who is wearing a pacemaker, place the electrode pads:

text: You have shocked a casualty and there are signs of circulation. You should:

optionA: A deep break in the skin involving significant bleeding.

text: Responders involved in wound management should consider:

text: Tetanus is a condition:

text: To prevent further contamination and infection of a wound, you should cleanse the surrounding skin by:
optionA: Swabbing in circular motions around the wound.
optionB: Wiping lightly over the edges of the wound.
optionC: Swabbing from one side of the wound to the other.

text: You are alone and must remove a casualty with a suspected spinal injury from a very hazardous situation. You should

text: "Referred pain":
optionA: Occurs when the sensation of pain is delayed.

optionD: Is the description of pain as recorded on the patient care report.

text: Trauma to male and female genitalia:

text: The priorities of MCIs include all but one of the following.
optionA: Overestimating the resources that may be required.

optionD: Arranging for advanced level care providers at the scene.

text: At the scene of a MCI:
optionA: All patients are assessed quickly and triaged.
optionC: Patients without a detectable pulse are given the highest priority.

text: Typically in a triage system:
optionA: Those whose survival requires immediate care are classified level 3.
optionB: Those with minor injuries but suffering extreme pain are classified level 1.
optionC: Those in cardiac arrest or not breathing are classified level 2.
optionD: Those who will survive if care is somewhat delayed are classified level 2.

text: The most knowledgable responder in the first ambulance is generally assigned to role of:
Of the following casualties, which would you care for first? The casualty who is:
optionA: In shock without apparent injuries, but conscious.
optionB: Unconscious and lying on his back.
optionC: Bleeding from the forehead, but conscious.
optionD: Unconscious and lying on his stomach.

text: The factor which indicates a drug related emergency is life-threatening is:
optionB: Dilated pupils slow to respond to light.
optionC: Lack of coordination and slurred speech.

text: Constant monitoring of the patient in a drug- related emergency is important because:
optionA: The patient will likely try to take more of the drug.
optionB: These patients are more likely to sue.
optionC: The patient’s condition may change quickly.
optionD: The patient is more likely to become violent. 29 Communications

text: The most accurate source of time for reporting purposes is:

optionD: The patient’s response to the care given

text: Which one of the following is the best way to assess dehydration in the elderly:
optionA: Check the mucous membranes in the eyes and mouth.

text: An eighty-five year old lady has fallen down a flight of stairs. You suspect spinal injury and decide to put the patient on a spineboard. You should be aware that:
optionA: It may be difficult to fit a cervical collar.
optionB: Spinal curvature may make it difficult and uncomfortable to put the patient on the board.
optionC: A K.E.D. is better for this patient.
optionD: Because of her age, she may be more prone to vomitting.

text: Which of the following is NOT a special consideration when assessing elderly patients:
optionA: Geriatric patients often have a reduced gag reflex.
optionB: Radial pulses may be weakened by peripheral vascular disease.
optionC: Patients may not show signs of fever even in cases of serious infection.
optionD: Mental status often reflects a fear of emergency responders. 31 Emergency Childbirth

text: Crowning occurs:
optionA: In the first stage of labour.
optionB: In the second stage of labour.
optionC: In the third stage of labour.
optionD: In the fourth stage of pregnancy.

text: When suctioning a newborn you should use:
To help control bleeding after the baby is born:
optionC: Elevate the mother’s legs above heart level.
optionD: Place the mother on her right side. 32 Pediatric Emergencies

text: One of the most difficult things to assess in infants and young children is:
The onset of shock in infants and children:
Assessments of children should take into consideration:
optionA: No splints should be used since all are designed for adult patients.
optionB: Abdominal injuries are less serious since muscles are less developed.
optionC: The skull has not completely fused, so head injuries are uncommon.
optionD: Chest injury is likely to involve organs without damage to ribs.

text: The responder who suspects a child to be the victim of abuse should:
optionB: Focus on management of injuries to the child.
optionD: Immediately remove the child from parents or caregivers.

text: A child with history of a sore throat, fever and painful swallowing, has breathing difficulties. You should:
optionA: Arrange for immediate transport to a medical facility.

text: The mammalian reflex refers to:
optionA: An increase in heart rate and respiration.
optionB: A decrease in heart rate and dilation of blood vessels.
optionC: An increase in heart rate and constriction of pupils.
optionD: A decrease in heart rate and constriction of blood vessels.

text: Three common scuba related emergencies are:
optionA: Air embolism, decompression sickness and mammalian reflex.
optionB: Air embolism, decompression sickness and barotrauma.
optionC: Decompression sickness, the bends and barotrauma.
optionD: Air embolism, barotrauma and the squeeze.

text: In cases of near drowning:
optionA: The lungs are often filled with water and require deep suctioning.
optionB: Laryngospasm prevents water from entering the lungs.
optionC: Abdominal thrusts should always be performed before ventilating the patient.
optionD: Manual suction devices are not powerful enough to remove water and fluids from the airway.

text: You have been helping at a water rescue when one of the divers begins to have problems. You suspect air embolism. This patient should receive:
To reduce the risk of respiratory infection and pneumonia where medical care is delayed, responders should:
optionA: Have the patient breathe deeply and cough.
optionB: Provide the patient with adequate hydration.
optionC: Position the patient in the position of comfort.
optionD: Ensure the patient is kept warm and at rest.

text: When positioning a patient, responders should consider that:
optionA: Raising arms and legs will produce swelling.
optionB: Semi-sitting will improve blood flow to the heart.
optionC: Knees raised will reduce pressure on the abdomen.
optionD: Recovery position will often improve breathing.

text: When caring for wounds over extended periods, always:
optionA: Remove dressings and bandages regularly to check the wound.
optionB: Clean wounds with antiseptic before applying dressings.
optionC: Apply a tourniquet to prevent excessive bleeding.
optionD: Check regularly for signs of infection or reduced circulation.

text: The maintenance of fluids and nutrition is key to extended patient care and can be provided by:
optionA: Giving fluids and food to all patients regardless of MOI.
optionB: Use small amounts of liquids and foods to determine whether the patient can tolerate the intake.
optionC: Allow the patient to consume any foods they would like to make them feel more comfortable.
optionD: Even if the patient is not thirsty or hungry the responder must insist on intake.

text: When administering medication, responders should consider that:
optionA: Right medication, right amount, right person, right time, right method.
optionB: Right nutrition, , right method, right time, right person, right amount
optionC: Right medication, right action, right person, right time, right responsibility
optionD: Right medication, right amount, right person, right responsibility, right action

text: To give ear drops to an adult, the ear should be pulled:
Provincial first aid legislation prescribes the contents of:
optionA: First aid boxes, first aid rooms and first aid manuals.
optionB: First aid stations, training programs and equipment manufacturers.
optionC: First aid boxes, first aid reports and record keeping.
optionD: First aid boxes, first aid rooms and training.

text: First aid stations must:
optionC: Be at least 6 square meters in size

text: Footprints can best be described as:
optionA: The area of contact between the road surface and the tires.
optionB: The type of tread on the tire.
optionC: Footsteps used to get into the back of the ambulance.

text: Ground guides or spotters should be situated:
optionA: Behind the vehicle on the driver side.
optionB: On the front left side of the vehicle.
optionC: Front and centre of the vehicle.
optionD: On the front right side of the vehicle.

text: When completing the patient narrative:
optionA: Try to establish an accurate diagnosis.
optionB: Record the signs and symptoms as accurately as possible.

text: A patient refuses your care. You should:
optionA: Fill out and personally sign a "Refusal of Care" form.
optionB: Proceed with patient assessment and management and ignore the refusal.
optionC: Find a relative or family member to give you consent.
optionD: Have the patient and a witness sign the "Refusal of Care" form.

text: Sublingual medication is given:
optionC: By inhalation using a metered dose inhaler.
optionD: As a tablet to be swallowed.

text: Contraindications of a medication tell responders:
optionA: When a medication should not be given to the patient.
optionB: When the medication is commonly used for patients.
optionC: The expected results of the medication.
optionD: Actions that might not be desirable yet occur along with desired effects.

text: The ACTION of the drug refers to:
To manage severe toothaches, responders should:
optionA: Advise the patient to chew on the other side of the mouth.
optionB: Advise the patient to drink hot liquids to sooth discomfort.
optionC: Advise the patient to suck on ice cubes to dull the pain.
optionD: Extract the tooth with the assistance of a second responder.

text: When handling a knocked-out tooth you should:
optionA: Place the tooth back in the patient's mouth.
optionB: Place the tooth in a cup of milk.
optionC: Wrap the tooth in dry gauze.
optionD: Place the tooth in a mild acidic solution using vinegar.

text: Lividity refers to:
optionA: Stiffening of the joints post mortem.
optionB: Cooling of the body post mortem.
optionC: Settling of blood in the body due to gravity.

text: Later signs of death include:
optionA: Staining, milky corneas and flushed skin.
optionC: Rigor mortis, lividity and milky corneas.
optionD: Rigor mortis, flushed skin and decomposition.
